# Epistemic Humility

## Explains

All models are approximations and confidence should be proportional to evidence,
not to how complete the model appears.

## The Concept

"Data points, not gospel truth." Every framework, every model, every standard is
a map — not the territory. Maps are useful precisely BECAUSE they simplify, but
simplification always distorts. The moment you confuse the map for the territory,
you stop noticing when reality diverges from your model.

The framework is upstream, but it's a living map that expects to be wrong in
places. Confidence comes from evidence (did it work in practice?) not from
completeness (does it cover all cases on paper?). A governance system that looks
comprehensive on paper but hasn't been tested against real ventures is less
trustworthy than one with known gaps that has survived contact with reality.

Systems that forget this become dogmatic. Dogmatic systems die when reality
shifts. The antidote is building revision mechanisms into the system itself —
pressure protocols, evidence-based evolution, stage labels that acknowledge
immaturity. Epistemic humility isn't weakness; it's the only honest stance when
operating in complex adaptive systems where certainty is an illusion.

The practical discipline: hold your models lightly. Test them against outcomes.
Update them when evidence demands it. Never mistake coverage for correctness.

## Why This Matters for Governance

Governance systems are particularly vulnerable to false confidence because they
deal in rules and standards — artifacts that LOOK authoritative by their nature.
A rule written in imperative language feels certain even when the reasoning
behind it is speculative. JOS must resist this trap.

Every JOS object carries a stage label, undergoes review cycles, and can be
challenged through the pressure protocol. These aren't bureaucratic overhead —
they're epistemic humility made structural. The system knows it might be wrong
and has built-in mechanisms to find out.

## Connects To

- JOS-P09 (Start Somewhere, Iterate)
- JOS-C11 (Evolutionary Optimization)
- Framework Pressure Protocol
